title: How Trauma Alters Pain Perception
researcherTopic: C-PTSD, the four F types, emotional flashbacks
metaDescription: Explore how complex trauma fundamentally alters pain perception by rewiring the nervous system. Learn why your body remembers what the mind forgets and how to begin healing.
imageDescription: Golden light filters through a window, illuminating the delicate, repaired cracks of a kintsugi bowl. The scene is quiet and still, evoking a sense of gentle mending and the beauty of a history that has been healed. The warmth of the light on the wooden table suggests a new morning and a peaceful beginning.
htmlContent:
The body’s memory of pain is not a metaphor. It is a physiological fact written into the nervous system, a ghost that haunts the architecture of our cells long after the original injury has passed, a silent hum beneath the surface of our days.

The Echo in the Tissues

When we experience trauma, particularly the developmental and relational trauma that leads to C-PTSD, the experience is not logged as a simple narrative in the brain’s neocortex. It bypasses the story-making mind and becomes a multi-sensory imprint, a constellation of unprocessed survival energy locked deep within the body’s tissues—the fascia, the muscles, the organs themselves. This is not merely a psychological phenomenon; it is a biological one. The nervous system, perpetually braced for a threat that has already passed, begins to operate on a different set of rules, a logic forged in crisis. It rewires itself for survival, and in doing so, it fundamentally alters its relationship with sensation itself, creating a new baseline for what is considered “normal.” A client once described this to me as feeling like their own body was a haunted house, where every creak and groan was a memory they couldn’t quite place but could feel in their bones. This is the lived reality for so many who navigate the world with a history of complex trauma—a world where the baseline of sensation is not neutral, but is instead tinged with the faint, persistent echo of danger, a low-grade hum of activation that never quite turns off.

This recalibration of the nervous system means that the perception of pain is no longer a straightforward signal of physical injury. It becomes a language, a dialect of the body’s ongoing conversation about safety and threat, a conversation that is happening largely outside of conscious awareness. The same stimulus that one person might register as minor discomfort—a slight ache in the back, a twinge in the knee—another, whose system is primed for danger, might experience as excruciating, a five-alarm fire of sensation. This is not an exaggeration or a weakness; it is the predictable outcome of a nervous system that has learned, through repeated and overwhelming experience, that the world is not a safe place. The work of healing, then, is not about ignoring or suppressing this pain, but about learning to understand its language, to see it not as an enemy to be defeated but as a messenger carrying vital information about the body’s deepest needs and oldest wounds. We can learn more about this intricate relationship in the context of The Body Keeps the Score, Revisited, which explores how these memories are held in the body.

The wellness industry sells solutions to problems it helps you believe you have.

The Nervous System as a Distorting Mirror

Imagine a mirror that, instead of reflecting your image accurately, subtly distorts it, making you appear taller, shorter, wider, or thinner than you actually are. Now imagine that you have been looking into this mirror your entire life, and you have come to believe that its reflection is the truth. This is what a traumatized nervous system does to sensation. It takes the raw data of physical experience and filters it through a lens of past danger, amplifying certain signals and dampening others, all in a desperate, intelligent attempt to keep you safe. This is the world of hypervigilance and dissociation, of chronic pain and numbness, of a body that is no longer a reliable home but a source of constant confusion and betrayal. In his seminal work on C-PTSD, Pete Walker speaks of the "four Fs"—fight, flight, freeze, and fawn—as the primary survival responses that become chronically activated. Each of these responses has a profound and distinct impact on how we experience our bodies and the world around us. A nervous system stuck in a "fight" or "flight" response, for example, will be flooded with adrenaline and cortisol, leading to a state of chronic muscular tension, inflammation, and digestive distress that can both create and exacerbate physical pain. A system caught in a "freeze" response, on the other hand, may lead to a sense of disconnection and numbness, a feeling of being detached from one’s own body, which can be just as disorienting and distressing as overt pain.

The journey of healing from this distorted perception is not about trying to shatter the mirror or force it to reflect a "normal" image. It is about learning to see the distortions for what they are—the intelligent adaptations of a system that has done its best to survive unbearable circumstances. It is about cultivating a relationship with the body that is based not on judgment or control, but on curiosity and compassion, a willingness to listen without an agenda. It is about learning to listen to the whispers of sensation beneath the roar of the pain, to find the subtle cues of safety and ease that are always present, even in the midst of the storm. This is the slow, patient work of retuning the nervous system, of teaching it, one moment at a time, that the war is over and that it is finally safe to come home. This process of recognizing and working with these ingrained patterns is often explored in the context of Understanding the Four F Responses.

Patience is not passive. It's the active practice of allowing something to unfold at its own pace.

When Numbness Becomes a Language

For many who have endured complex trauma, the opposite of pain is not pleasure, but numbness. This is not a passive absence of feeling, but an active, protective mechanism of the nervous system, a form of self-anesthesia. When the intensity of an experience is too much for the system to bear, it can shut down, creating a buffer of dissociation that allows us to survive the unsurvivable. This can outcome as a sense of unreality, a feeling of being detached from one’s own body or emotions, or a generalized lack of sensation, a world rendered in shades of gray. While this numbness can be a necessary and intelligent survival strategy in the moment, it can become a chronic pattern that disconnects us from our own vitality, our joy, our creativity, and from the world around us. In my years of working in this territory, I have seen how this numbness can be one of the most insidious and difficult aspects of trauma to heal. It is a silent suffering, a pain that is defined by its own absence, a void where a self should be.

Learning to work with this numbness requires a profound shift in perspective, a movement away from force and towards finesse. Instead of trying to "break through" it or force ourselves to feel, we must learn to approach it with a gentle and patient curiosity, as if approaching a frightened animal. We must learn to listen to the silence, to feel into the emptiness, to trust that even in the most barren landscapes of our inner world, there is life waiting to be discovered. This is the work of somatic inquiry, of bringing a kind and non-judgmental attention to the subtle sensations of the body—the slight shift in temperature, the faint vibration, the gentle rhythm of the breath. It is a process of titration, of learning to experience small, manageable amounts of sensation at a time, so that the nervous system can gradually build its capacity to feel without becoming overwhelmed. This delicate process is a central theme in the practice of Somatic Experiencing for Trauma.

The Alchemy of Attention

The quality of our attention is the most powerful tool we have for reshaping our experience of pain. Where we place our focus, and how we place it, can either amplify our suffering or create the conditions for its transformation. In the context of a traumatized nervous system, attention often becomes hyper-focused on the site of the pain, a narrow, rigid beam of awareness that gets stuck on the sensation, magnifying it and reinforcing the brain’s perception of threat. We become fused with the pain, believing that we *are* the pain. The work of healing involves learning to wield our attention with more skill and wisdom, to broaden the beam, to include not just the pain, but the space around the pain, the parts of the body that feel neutral or even pleasant, the gentle movement of the breath, the sounds in the room. This is not a form of distraction, but of expansion. It is a way of reminding the nervous system that the pain is not the whole story, that there is more to our experience than just this one sensation.

This practice of mindful awareness, of bringing a spacious and non-judgmental attention to our inner world, is a form of alchemy. It does not necessarily make the pain go away, but it changes our relationship to it. It creates a space between the sensation and our reaction to it, and in that space, we find a new kind of freedom. We begin to see that the pain is just a sensation, a transient event in the vast field of our awareness. We learn to meet it with curiosity instead of fear, with compassion instead of resistance. And in doing so, we begin to untangle the knot of physical sensation, emotional reactivity, and catastrophic thinking that creates so much of our suffering. This is the path of dis-identification, of realizing that we are not the pain, but the awareness that is holding the pain. This is a foundational concept in many contemplative traditions, and it is a key to unlocking a new way of being with our bodies and ourselves, a topic we explore further in Mindfulness and Trauma Healing.

Freedom is not the absence of constraint. It's the capacity to choose your relationship to it.

The Architecture of a New Reality

Healing from the way trauma alters pain perception is not about returning to a previous state of being, a mythical “before” that likely never existed in the way we remember it. The person we were before the trauma is gone, and the attempt to reclaim that self is a recipe for frustration and despair. Instead, healing is about building a new reality, a new relationship with our bodies and our sensations, one that is informed by the wisdom we have gained through our experiences. It is about learning to inhabit a nervous system that is not defined by its past, but is instead capable of responding to the present moment with flexibility and resilience. This is not a quick or easy process. It is a journey of a thousand small steps, of learning to trust the body’s innate intelligence, of discovering that we have the capacity to create our own safety from within. It is a process of construction, not restoration.

This process of building a new reality involves a multi-faceted approach, a weaving together of different threads of healing. It involves the cognitive work of understanding the impact of trauma on the nervous system, as we can learn from external resources like Psychology Today, which offers a wealth of articles on the subject. It involves the somatic work of learning to listen to the body’s language of sensation, of tracking the subtle shifts and changes that signal a movement towards regulation. And it involves the relational work of learning to connect with others in a way that feels safe and supportive, of co-regulating with trusted friends, partners, or therapists. It is about creating a life that is not a reaction to the past, but an expression of our deepest values and our most authentic desires. It is about discovering that we are not broken, but have instead been reshaped by our experiences into something stronger, more compassionate, and more whole. We can find more information on the biological underpinnings of this from resources like the National Center for Biotechnology Information.

The Topography of Inner Safety

One of the most profound shifts in the journey of healing from complex trauma is the discovery that safety is not something that can be found in the external world. It is not a place we can go or a person we can be with. It is a state of being that we must cultivate within ourselves, a felt sense in the body. This is the work of creating an internal landscape of safety, a topography of the soul that is rich with resources and refuge. It is about learning to identify the people, places, and practices that nourish our nervous systems and to orient our lives around them. It is about learning to set boundaries, to say no to that which depletes us and yes to that which enlivens us. It is about learning to be our own best advocate, our own most compassionate friend, the parent we never had.

This inner safety is not a static destination, but a dynamic and ongoing practice, a moment-to-moment choice. It is a choice to turn towards ourselves with kindness and curiosity, to listen to the needs of our bodies, and to respond to them with wisdom and care. It is about learning to find the ground of our own being, to feel the support of the earth beneath us, and to know, in our bones, that we are held. This is the essence of embodiment, of learning to live in a way that is deeply connected to the wisdom of the body, a wisdom that has been there all along, waiting for us to listen. It is a journey of coming home to ourselves, of discovering that the safety we have been searching for has been here all along, waiting patiently for us to arrive. This is a journey that many of us are on, and we can find solidarity in knowing that we are not alone in this, as we can see in discussions around Building a Life After Trauma.

There's a meaningful difference between self-improvement and self-understanding. One adds. The other reveals.

You have read the information. You understand the mechanisms. You can explain the theory to someone else. None of that matters if you do not put your body where your understanding is. Knowledge without embodiment is just another defense mechanism. A sophisticated one, but a defense mechanism nonetheless.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does my physical pain get worse when I'm stressed?

When you're stressed, your body releases hormones like cortisol and adrenaline, which can increase inflammation and muscle tension, making existing pain feel more intense. For a nervous system shaped by trauma, this response is often amplified, as the brain interprets emotional stress as a physical threat, further heightening pain sensitivity and activating old patterns of bracing and defense.

Can healing trauma get rid of my chronic pain?

While there's no guarantee, many people find that as they heal from trauma and learn to regulate their nervous system, their chronic pain significantly lessens or even resolves. The process is less about "getting rid of" the pain and more about changing your relationship to it, untangling the physical sensation from the emotional charge, which in turn can change the experience of it.

What is a simple first step to reconnect with my body?

A simple and profound first step is to practice noticing. A few times a day, pause and bring your attention to a part of your body that feels neutral or even pleasant—perhaps the feeling of your feet on the floor, the warmth of your hands, or the gentle expansion and contraction of your belly as you breathe. This gentle, non-demanding attention can begin to rebuild a sense of safety and trust in your body.

The path of healing is not a straight line, but a spiral, a dance of returning again and again to the places within ourselves that need our attention and our care. It is a journey of learning to be with the full spectrum of our experience, the pain and the pleasure, the light and the dark, with a heart that is open and a spirit that is free. We can learn more about this journey from a teacher like Kalesh, who reminds us that the goal is not to be free from pain, but to be free from our resistance to it.

Here is the uncomfortable truth: understanding changes nothing without practice.
